sentence: Subjective:
The patient, Angela, presents with a history of recurrent kidney stones, with this being her seventh or eighth episode.
She reports that the current stone has passed, and she is no longer experiencing pain or blood in her urine.
She also has a past medical history of hypertension and diabetes, for which she is currently taking 2.5 mg of Norvasc and 500 mg of metformin daily.
Her blood pressure was 150/90 mmHg at the time of her visit.
She admits to having difficulty controlling her diet, particularly with regard to fast food and sweets, and has not been checking her blood sugar levels regularly.
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 150/90 mmHg, pulse rate not mentioned, oxygen saturation not mentioned, temperature not mentioned.
Physical exam: The lungs are clear bilaterally, and the heart exam reveals a grade 2 out of 6 systolic ejection murmur, which has not worsened since her previous visit.
The abdomen exam is notable for no tenderness to palpation on the right side.
Test Results: [NOT MENTIONED]
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a history of recurrent kidney stones, hypertension, and diabetes, and is currently doing well on her current medications.
Problem list: Kidney stones (resolved); Hypertension (controlled); Diabetes (controlled).
Plan:
I prescribed Norvasc 2.5 mg daily for hypertension.
I gave her a prescription refill for Norvasc as needed.
I prescribed a consult to a nutritionist to help her with her diet.
I also prescribed 500 mg of metformin daily for diabetes and advised her to start taking her blood sugar levels before meals.
I gave her a referral to urology to evaluate her recurrent kidney stones.
I prescribed 10 mg of melatonin as needed for insomnia.
Follow-up:
I will see the patient in three months to re-evaluate her hypertension, diabetes, and kidney stones.
